name: Bukhara
description: Bukhara is over 2,500 years old — one of Uzbekistan’s holiest ancient cities, the “City of Domes”. A UNESCO World Heritage historic centre, hub of Islamic culture, with the Ark fortress, Kalon Minaret, many madrasas and major tourism. Ibn Sina, Bahauddin Naqshband and others lived and worked here; today it draws visitors from around the world.

monuments.title: Ark fortress
monuments.info: The city’s oldest monumental citadel on an artificial hill (4 ha, 16–20 m); rulers’ “city within a city” and residence of Bukhara emirs until 1920.

monuments.title: Poi Kalon
monuments.info: 12th–16th c. ensemble: Kalon Minaret, Kalon Mosque and Mir-i-Arab Madrasa. The minaret served caravans as a landmark and survived the Mongol invasion.

monuments.title: Lyabi Hauz
monuments.info: 16th–17th c. complex around Nadir Divanbegi’s pool: Kukeldash and Nadir Divanbegi madrasas and a khanaka — the heart of the old centre.

monuments.title: Ismail Samani Mausoleum
monuments.info: c. 892–907: tomb of Ismail Samani and dynasty; masterpiece of baked-brick geometry, Sogdian tradition, UNESCO-listed.

monuments.title: Chor Minor
monuments.info: 1807 madrasa with four distinctive towers; some see symbolic reference to world faiths; colourful decor in the historic centre.

monuments.title: Sitorai Mohi Hosa
monuments.info: 19th–20th c. summer palace of Bukhara emirs (“Star-like the Moon”); north of the city, blend of Eastern and European styles; museum of decorative arts and wellness centre.

monuments.title: Bolo Hauz Mosque
monuments.info: 1712, “Forty Columns” mosque opposite the Ark; emirs attended Friday prayer here. Twenty tall wooden pillars reflect in the pool — forty visible.

monuments.title: Ulugh Beg and Abdulaziz Khan madrasas
monuments.info: Facing pair (1417 and 1652): astronomical decoration vs rich ornament — highlights of medieval Islamic education and architecture.

monuments.title: Magoki Attori Mosque
monuments.info: 12th–16th c. domed mosque partly below ground; famous carved decoration; among Bukhara’s oldest surviving monuments.

monuments.title: Chor Bakr necropolis
monuments.info: 16th c. “City of the Dead” near Bukhara: tombs of Juybari sheikhs; mosque, madrasa, khanaka and courtyards in Sumitan village.

monuments.title: Chashma Ayub Mausoleum
monuments.info: 12th–16th c. over a sacred spring linked to Prophet Job; conical domes; today a water museum.

monuments.title: Abdulkhaliq Gijduvani shrine (1st pir)
monuments.info: Abdulkhaliq Gijduvani (1103–1179), first of the seven pirs, founder of Khojagan; complex in Gijduvan with mausoleum, madrasa and mosque.

monuments.title: Khoja Orif Revgari shrine (2nd pir)
monuments.info: Khoja Orif ar-Rivgari, second pir, major mystic in the formation of Khojagan–Naqshbandi tradition.

monuments.title: Khoja Mahmud Anjir Faghnavi (3rd pir)
monuments.info: d. 1317, third pir, great Naqshbandi sheikh; complex with a revered well.

monuments.title: Khoja Ali Romitaniy (4th pir)
monuments.info: 1191–1321, fourth pir, known as Hazrati Azizon in Sufi circles.

monuments.title: Khoja Muhammad Boboyi Samosi (5th pir)
monuments.info: 1259–1354, fifth pir; linked to Bahauddin Naqshband’s spiritual lineage.

monuments.title: Sayyid Amir Kulol (6th pir)
monuments.info: In Kofirshon village: patron of potters and spiritual teacher of Bahauddin Naqshband.

monuments.title: Bahauddin Naqshband (7th pir)
monuments.info: Great complex for the founder of the Naqshbandi order — among Islam’s holiest pilgrimage sites, also called Qasri Arifon.
